title: What is a data type
instruction:
Think about a moving truck.

You would not put liquid in a cardboard box. You would not put a piano in a plastic bag. Different things need different containers.

Data works the same way. Text needs one kind of container. A whole number needs another. A yes/no answer needs another.

In Go, these containers are called data types. Every variable has one.

Run this and see three different types in action.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name := "Alex"
	age := 25
	active := true
	fmt.Println(name)
	fmt.Println(age)
	fmt.Println(active)
}
successMessage: Three variables, three types. Each one holds a different kind of data.

title: string — text
instruction:
A `string` holds text. Any text. A single letter, a word, a sentence, an email address.

You always wrap it in double quotes. That is how Go knows it is text and not code.

Create a variable called `email` with any email address as a string. Then print it.
hint: email := "you@example.com"  — text always goes inside double quotes.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Strings are the most common type you will use. Any text, always in double quotes.

title: int — whole numbers
instruction:
`int` holds whole numbers. No decimal point. Ages, counts, scores, years.

When you just write a plain number without a decimal, Go gives it the type `int` by default.

`int` adjusts to the computer it runs on — 64-bit on modern machines, 32-bit on older ones. For most programs, `int` is all you need.

Create a variable called `year` with the current year. Print it.
hint: year := 2025  — no quotes, no decimal point.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: int is the default number type. It covers most use cases.

title: int8, int16, int32, int64 — sized integers
instruction:
Sometimes you need to control exactly how much memory a number uses. That is what sized integers are for.

The number after `int` is how many bits it uses — and bits determine the range:

- `int8`  — 8 bits  — range: -128 to 127
- `int16` — 16 bits — range: -32,768 to 32,767
- `int32` — 32 bits — range: -2 billion to 2 billion
- `int64` — 64 bits — range: very very large numbers

Smaller types use less memory. Larger types hold bigger numbers. You pick based on what your data needs.

Run this to see them all.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var small  int8  = 100
	var medium int16 = 30000
	var large  int32 = 2000000000
	var huge   int64 = 9000000000000
	fmt.Println(small)
	fmt.Println(medium)
	fmt.Println(large)
	fmt.Println(huge)
}
successMessage: Same idea as int but with a specific size. Use the smallest one that fits your data.

title: uint — unsigned integers
instruction:
Regular `int` can hold negative numbers. But sometimes your value can never be negative — a file size, a pixel count, an ID number.

For those, use `uint` (unsigned integer). It only holds zero and positive numbers, which means it can hold twice the positive range.

- `uint8`  — 0 to 255
- `uint16` — 0 to 65,535
- `uint32` — 0 to 4 billion
- `uint64` — 0 to very very large

Add a `var` declaration for `pixelCount` as `uint32` with the value `1920`, then print it.

codeChecks.message: Declare pixelCount as uint32: var pixelCount uint32 = 1920
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: uint is int without the negative side. Use it when your value can never go below zero.

title: float32 and float64 — decimal numbers
instruction:
When a whole number is not precise enough, you need a float.

- `float32` — 32 bits, about 7 decimal digits of precision
- `float64` — 64 bits, about 15 decimal digits of precision

Always use `float64` unless you have a specific reason not to. It is more precise and it is what Go uses by default when it sees a decimal number.

Create a `temperature` variable as `float32` with value `36.6`, and a `distance` variable as `float64` with value `12345.6789`. Print both.
hint: var temperature float32 = 36.6  and  var distance float64 = 12345.6789

codeChecks.message: Declare temperature as float32: var temperature float32 = 36.6
Declare distance as float64: var distance float64 = 12345.6789
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: float64 is the one you will use almost every time. More precision, same effort.

title: bool — true or false
instruction:
A `bool` holds exactly one of two values: `true` or `false`.

That is it. Nothing else fits.

No quotes. `true` and `false` are keywords in Go — not text, not numbers.

Create two bool variables: `isOnline` set to `true`, and `isPremium` set to `false`. Print both.
hint: isOnline := true  and  isPremium := false  — no quotes around true or false.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Bools power every decision in your program. Every if, every condition, every check — it all comes down to true or false.

title: byte — a single character as a number
instruction:
`byte` is an alias for `uint8`. It holds a number from 0 to 255.

Its main job is representing a single character. Every character on your keyboard has a number assigned to it — that system is called ASCII.

- `'A'` = 65
- `'a'` = 97
- `'0'` = 48

Notice single quotes for a character, not double quotes.

Run this and see what number the letter 'G' maps to.
successMessage: 'G' is 71 in ASCII. Every character your computer shows on screen is secretly a number.

title: rune — a Unicode character
instruction:
`rune` is an alias for `int32`. It holds a single Unicode character.

The difference from `byte`:
- `byte` handles ASCII — basic English letters and symbols (0-255)
- `rune` handles Unicode — every character in every language on earth (over 1 million possible values)

If your program will ever work with non-English text — Arabic, Chinese, emoji — you need `rune`.

Run this and see the Unicode value of the character.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var letter rune = '\u0041'
	var emoji  rune = '\U0001F600'
	fmt.Println(letter)
	fmt.Println(emoji)
}
successMessage: rune handles the entire world's writing systems. byte handles the basics. Now you know the difference.

title: Type inference — Go figures it out
instruction:
You have been using `:=` without ever writing the type name. That is because Go looks at the value and figures out the type on its own.

This is called type inference.

`name := "Alex"` → Go sees quotes → `string`
`age := 25` → Go sees a whole number → `int`
`price := 4.99` → Go sees a decimal → `float64`
`active := true` → Go sees true/false → `bool`

You can print the actual type of any variable using `%T` in `fmt.Printf`.

Run this and see the types Go assigned.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name   := "Alex"
	age    := 25
	price  := 4.99
	active := true
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", name)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", age)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", price)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", active)
}
successMessage: Go reads your value and picks the right type automatically. You only need to be explicit when you want a specific sized type.

title: "42" is not 42
instruction:
This is a trap that catches a lot of beginners.

`42` is a number. You can do math with it.
`"42"` is a string — the characters 4 and 2. You cannot do math with it.

They look similar but Go treats them as completely different things.

This program has a bug because of this exact mistake. Fix it so both numbers add up correctly.
hint: Remove the quotes around 42 on the second line. A number should never have quotes around it.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	a := 10
	b := "42"
	fmt.Println(a + b)
}

codeChecks.message: Remove the quotes from 42. It should be a number: b := 42

successMessage: Quotes mean text. No quotes means number. Never mix them.

title: Type conversion — changing from one type to another
instruction:
Go will not automatically mix types. If you want to use an `int` where a `float64` is needed, you have to convert it yourself.

The syntax is simple: wrap the value with the type name.

`float64(myInt)` converts an int to float64
`int(myFloat)` converts a float64 to int (drops the decimal)
`int64(myInt)` converts int to int64

The code below tries to add an `int` and a `float64` directly. Fix it by converting `score` to `float64` before the addition.
hint: Change the Println line to: fmt.Println(float64(score) + bonus)
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	score := 95
	bonus := 4.5
	fmt.Println(score + bonus)
}

codeChecks.message: Convert score to float64 before adding: float64(score) + bonus

successMessage: Go never converts types silently. You are always in control of when and how a conversion happens.

title: Types do not change
instruction:
Once a variable has a type, it keeps that type forever.

You cannot store a string in an int variable. You cannot store a float in a bool variable. Go will refuse and give you an error.

This program tries to do exactly that. Read the error, understand why, then fix it by changing the second assignment to a valid int value.
hint: score is an int. You cannot assign "one hundred" (a string) to it. Change it to the number 100.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	score := 95
	fmt.Println(score)
	score = "one hundred"
	fmt.Println(score)
}

codeChecks.message: score is an int. Replace "one hundred" with the number 100.

successMessage: Types are permanent. That rigidness is actually a feature — it catches a whole class of bugs before your program ever runs.

title: Use six types in one program
instruction:
No starter code this time.

Create six variables using six different types:
- `username` — string
- `level` — int
- `score` — int64
- `accuracy` — float64
- `isActive` — bool
- `grade` — byte (set it to `'A'`)

Print all six.
hint: username := "...", level := 1, var score int64 = 1000, accuracy := 98.5, isActive := true, var grade byte = 'A'

codeChecks.message: Create score as int64 using the var keyword
Create accuracy as a float64 with a decimal value
Create grade as a byte set to a letter like 'A'

successMessage: Six types, one program. You now know every basic data type in Go.
